text: Montaigne, writing in his tower study in the 1570s, argued that solitude is not the same as physical isolation. A man who flees the city but carries his ambitions and resentments with him has achieved nothing. 'We must reserve a little back-shop,' he wrote, 'all our own, entirely free, in which to establish our real liberty.' The back-shop is an interior room — a habitual retreat where the mind decouples from social performance and recovers its own judgments. Montaigne's tower was a symbol of this inner withdrawal, not its cause.
question: What does Montaigne mean by the 'back-shop' of the mind?

options.text: A private library stocked with classical texts
An interior mental space reserved for free, unperformed thought
The habit of writing in solitude every morning
A physical room separate from the household
